text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
Yesterday we studied the Golden Altar of Incense in detail; what it was, where it stood, how it was used, who tended it, and how it was covered and prepared for the Kohathites to carry. Today we look at what every part of that altar was pointing to. Now, since we have already studied the acacia wood and the gold in depth when we looked at the Ark, the Table of Showbread, and the Lampstand, we will not go over those again. We already know that the wood speaks of a character that does not decay under pressure, and the gold speaks of the divine character in which Christ walked. Today we look at the other features of the incense altar and what each one reveals about Christ and His ministry.

Of all the furniture in the Holy Place, the Golden Altar of Incense stood the closest to the veil. The Lampstand was further back. The Table of Showbread was against the north wall. But the altar of incense was placed directly before the curtain that separated the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place, where the Ark and the Mercy Seat resided. Exodus 30:6 records God’s instruction precisely: it was to stand “before the veil that is above the ark of the testimony, before the mercy seat.” So close was the altar to the Most Holy Place that in Hebrews 9:3-4 it is described in connection with the inner sanctuary, even though it physically stood in the outer room. It was the piece of furniture nearest to the heart of God.

This position speaks of the intercessory ministry of Christ, which stands perpetually before the very presence of Yahweh. Hebrews 9:24 says: “For Christ has entered, not into holy places made with hands, which are copies of the true things, but into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God on our behalf.” The incense altar in the tabernacle stood before the veil; at the threshold of the presence. Christ entered through the veil into the presence itself. The altar was as close as the earthly tabernacle could place anything to God. Christ went beyond the veil entirely. His intercession is not at the threshold. It is at the throne. Romans 8:34 says: “Christ Jesus is the one who died more than that, who was raised who is at the right hand of God, who indeed is interceding for us.” The right hand of God is not near the veil. It is on the other side of it.

The incense on the golden altar was not burned occasionally. It was not burned on special days only or when the people felt they needed it. Exodus 30:7-8 commanded Aaron to burn incense every morning when he tended the lamps and every evening when he lit them again. Morning and evening. Every day. Without exception. When the morning incense was exhausted, the evening incense was lit. There was never a gap. There was never a day when the fragrance stopped rising before Yahweh. The incense on the golden altar was the Lechem Tamid of prayer; the continual, unbroken, perpetual rising of the people’s worship and intercession before the face of God.

This speaks of the intercession of Christ with breathtaking precision.
Hebrews 7:25 is the key verse: “Consequently, He is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through Him, since He always lives to make intercession for them.” Always. Not in the morning only. Not on the appointed days. Always. Right now, as you read these words, Christ is interceding for you before the Father. There has not been a single moment since His resurrection and ascension when the incense of His intercession has not been rising before Yahweh. The morning incense was never left unlit.
The evening incense was never forgotten. And the intercession of Christ has never once paused, cooled, or gone silent. The altar burns continuously because the Intercessor lives continuously.

The timing of the incense burning is also significant. It was burned at the same time as the lamps of the lampstand were tended morning and evening.
The incense and the light always rose together. John Gill, commenting on this, wrote: “He that was seen amidst the golden candlesticks dressing the lamps, appears at the golden altar with a golden censer, to offer up the prayers of His saints.” The one who is the light of the world is also the one who carries the prayers of the world before the Father. The ministry of light and the ministry of intercession belong to the same person. Christ is both the Lampstand and the Altar the one who illuminates and the one who intercedes.

The incense that burned on the golden altar was not ordinary incense. It was a unique, sacred blend of four specific spices stacte, onycha, galbanum, and pure frankincense in exactly equal portions, salted and purified (Exodus 30:34-35). This formula belonged exclusively to Yahweh. It could not be reproduced for any other use. Verse 37-38 adds a solemn warning: whoever made the same formula for their own pleasure would be cut off from the people.
The fragrance that rose before God was entirely set apart from everything in the world. It was unique, irreplaceable, and holy.

This incense speaks of Christ as a fragrant offering before the Father. Ephesians 5:2 says He “gave Himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God.” His entire life every prayer, every act of obedience, every moment of holiness in an unholy world was the rising fragrance of the incense before Yahweh.
And just as the incense formula was unique and could not be copied or replicated, the ministry of Christ cannot be replicated or replaced by any human religion, any human prayer system, or any other mediator. John 14:6 says: “I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.” The formula is not transferable. The fragrance that reaches God is Christ alone. Hebrews 13:15 says our sacrifice of praise is offered “through Him” always through Him, never around Him, never in spite of Him.

The prayers of the people that the incense represented are described in Revelation 5:8 as “golden bowls full of incense, which are the prayers of the saints,” and in Revelation 8:3-4 an angel stands at the golden altar “with much incense to offer with the prayers of all the saints on the golden altar before the throne, and the smoke of the incense, with the prayers of the saints, rose before God from the hand of the angel.” The prayers of God’s people do not reach the Father by themselves. They are mingled with the incense of Christ’s intercession and offered together. Our prayers are imperfect, broken, incomplete. But they are carried to the throne in the golden bowl of Christ’s own fragrant intercession, and they arrive before God acceptable and heard.

title: Prayer
text: Yahweh, there is no one like You. You are the God who hears prayer, to You all flesh shall come (Psalm 65:2). We thank You that You did not leave us without a golden altar that You placed the ministry of intercession at the threshold of Your presence and then, in Christ, opened the way through the veil entirely. We thank You that the incense never runs out that the morning and evening of Your Son’s intercession never have a gap, that there is no hour of any day when He is not presenting us before Your face. We thank You that our broken, imperfect prayers do not rise to You alone; but are mingled with the fragrance of Christ and carried before Your throne acceptable and heard. Teach us to pray. Teach us to bring our prayers to the golden altar, not trusting in our own words but in the intercession of the one who always lives to make intercession for us. In Christ’s name, amen.
